text:
$IONQ The military use case for quantum technology is becoming one of the most important investment themes in the sector.

Most investors focus on quantum computing. Governments and defense agencies are increasingly focused on quantum sensing, quantum networking, secure communications, and GPS-independent navigation.

According to recent analysis from the Center for Strategic and International Studies, quantum sensing has the potential to reshape modern warfare by enabling detection capabilities that conventional systems struggle to achieve. Applications include:

• Detecting submarines through quantum magnetometers
• Identifying underground tunnels and hidden infrastructure through quantum gravimeters
• GPS-denied navigation using atomic clocks and inertial sensors
• Enhanced intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR)
• Quantum-secure communications and networking
The strategic implication is simple:
Many of today's military advantages depend on remaining undetected.

If quantum sensing matures as expected, stealth aircraft, submarines, underground facilities, and even electronic warfare tactics could become more vulnerable to detection. CSIS compares the potential impact to the way radar transformed warfare in the 20th century.

This is where $IONQ becomes particularly interesting.
Most people still classify $IONQ as a pure-play quantum computing company. However, the company has spent the last two years building a broader quantum technology platform spanning:

• Quantum computing
• Quantum networking
• Quantum security
• Quantum sensing
• Government and defense applications

A major development was the acquisition of Vector Atomic, a company with more than $200 million in government contracts and technologies including:

• Atomic clocks
• Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) systems
• Gravimeters
• Inertial sensors

These technologies are directly applicable to defense, aerospace, and national security programs. Vector Atomic technology has also been associated with military and space applications, including programs supporting the U.S. Department of Defense.

$IONQ has also delivered quantum networking systems to the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory and established IonQ Federal to serve U.S. government and allied defense customers. Importantly, $IONQ is not the only company positioned for this trend.
Other notable players include:
• $IBM — quantum computing and government research partnerships
• $QNT — quantum computing, cybersecurity, and defense-oriented research
• $RGTI — participant in U.S. government quantum initiatives 
• $QBTS — optimization and defense-related research applications
• $QUBT — photonics and sensing technologies
• Q-CTRL — quantum navigation systems designed for GPS-denied environments
• SandboxAQ — quantum sensing, cybersecurity, and government applications

The market may be underestimating a key point:
The first trillion-dollar quantum opportunity may not be quantum computing.

It may be the deployment of quantum sensing, timing, navigation, communications, and networking systems across defense, intelligence, aerospace, and critical infrastructure in sync with commercial use as well. The way I see it today, $IONQ seems to be the most structured company to date with the most resources, which is critical to deploy military related capabilities. 

If that thesis proves correct, $IONQ's expansion beyond computing and into sensing, networking, and federal programs could be one of the most significant strategic moves in the entire quantum industry. If you don't think Quantum will have military applications I recommend to look again...

text:
$GFS quietly continues to act well.

🤖 AI infrastructure, silicon photonics, co-packaged optics, and physical AI remain powerful tailwinds.

📈 After an 85% run, the stock is doing exactly what you’d want to see — consolidating above rising moving averages instead of giving back gains.

🎯 Recent shakeouts appear to be finding support near the 21MA, with price now attempting to reclaim the short-term trend.

👀 A move back through the recent highs could put the next leg higher in play.

Not many stocks can make a big move, consolidate, and still hold above the 10MA, 21MA, 50MA, and 200MA.

$GFS $NVDA $AVGO $MRVL $AMKR $TSM $LITE $CIEN 📈

text:
And here's the connection most investors haven't made yet:

The enabling technology for orbital infrastructure is photonics.

The same five-layer supply chain moving AI data at the speed of light inside terrestrial data centres is the same stack being positioned for space-based infrastructure.

text:
Memory and Photonics get most of the attention.

But there's a third constraint forming that I believe is still almost entirely under the radar.
The terrestrial grid.

AI data centres require 50–200 megawatts per facility. US grid interconnection queues now run five to ten years. There is no meaningful new power available at scale before 2030.

This is not speculation. It's a permitting and infrastructure timeline problem.

That’s the leg that gets repriced when token bills get reined in.

The AI trade is a whole infrastructure stack. 👇 https://t.co/gnRCD1yJui

First it was GPUs. Then memory. Now it’s PHOTONICS → and demand exceeds supply until mid-2027. (sound familiar?👀)

Every AI rack is a chain: compute → memory → the optics that move the data between them. As copper hits its physical wall at 1.6T, the bottleneck moves to light. https://t.co/R09NKqT1Xd

The bottleneck already moved. A GPU that computes in nanoseconds and waits microseconds for data is a stranded asset. At 1.6T speeds, copper runs out of physics. The constraint on AI is no longer how fast you can think. It's how fast you can move what you thought. Jensen has now said it twice in three months. At GTC in March: "Is copper going to still be important? The answer is yes... Are you going to scale up optical? Yes. Are you going to scale out optical? Yes... We need a lot more capacity for copper. We need a lot more capacity for optics. We need a lot more capacity for CPO." Last week at Computex, on Marvell's stage: "Optics where you must, copper where you can." Then he called Marvell the next trillion-dollar company and the optical complex repriced within days. The same keynote put a date on the handoff: 200G per lane is the last generation where copper is sufficient. After that, optics takes the rack. Translation: not copper OR light. Copper now, light next, unprecedented amounts of both. 🔥 The chain is unavoidable: AI tokens are profitable → more GPUs → more bandwidth → copper hits its wall → photonics becomes the chokepoint. And the smart money stopped debating. Follow the closed deals: → $NVDA has committed at least $6.5B to photonics in three months: $2B into Lumentum, $2B into Coherent, a $500M stake in Corning, and a piece of Ayar Labs' $500M round. Direct investments to secure its own light supply. → $MRVL paid $3.25B for Celestial AI, up to $5.5B with milestones, to build what its CEO calls a silicon photonics powerhouse. → $CRDO closed DustPhotonics two weeks ago. Ciena bought CPO startup Nubis for $270M. North of $10B of strategic capital locked up one supply chain in under a year. Capital like that doesn't chase a theme. It secures a bottleneck. LAYER 1: WAFER. Every laser starts as a crystal. 🟠 $AXTI: the InP substrate leader. The first chokepoint in the stack. 🟡 $IQE: compound-semi epiwafers feeding the laser makers. Speculative, but structurally upstream. LAYER 2: LIGHT. Photons don't make themselves. 🟠 $LITE: revenue +90% YoY last quarter to $808M. EML shipments doubled and management says demand still exceeds supply across EMLs, pump lasers, and transceivers. NVIDIA just wired them $2B. OCS backlog past $400M plus a multi-hundred-million CPO order for 2027. 🟢 $SIVEF (Stockholm: SIVE): the external light source. CPO does not emit its own light. Every optical engine needs a continuous-wave InP laser feeding it, and that is the layer you cannot engineer around. ELS modules with POET hit production readiness end of this year. Disclosure: long. 🟣 $POET: the optical engine wildcard. Its Optical Interposer pairs with Sivers' lasers on external light sources for CPO, with a LITEON module deal stacked on top. Binary commercialization, real architecture. LAYER 3: OPTICS AND MODULES. Where light meets the rack. 🟠 $COHR: the volume anchor in transceivers, holding NVIDIA's other $2B check. 🔵 $AAOI: Q1 revenue +51% to a record $151M, datacenter revenue more than doubled, $124M of 800G orders plus a $200M+ 1.6T order in hand. Scaling Texas capacity toward 500K+ units a month by year-end, targeting $1B+ revenue this year. Domestic supply while everyone fights over offshore. Disclosure: long. 🟠 $FN: the foundry of optics. When Fabrinet is building, the orders already exist. THE INTERCONNECT: the layer the rack cannot route around. 🔵 $CRDO: just closed DustPhotonics. SerDes → DSP → silicon photonics → system integration, one company, 800G through 3.2T. Electrical AND optical, end to end. FY26 revenue tripled to $1.34B at 68% gross margin. The toll booth on both roads. Disclosure: long. 🟠 $MRVL: $3.25B for Celestial AI, and Jensen's trillion-dollar nod on the Computex stage. 🟠 $AVGO: switch silicon, optical DSPs, CPO engines. They define the socket. 🟠 $ANET: the AI spine. 100K-GPU clusters get stitched together in light. LAYER 4: PACKAGING, FIBER, FOUNDRY. Where photons get industrialized. 🔵 $TSEM: the neutral silicon photonics foundry. Prints wafers for whoever wins. 🟣 $LPKF: glass-substrate packaging for glass-based CPO. Real technology, binary commercialization. 🟠 $GLW: AI racks demand several times the fiber density of legacy cloud, and NVIDIA just took a $500M stake. Corning sells density. LAYER 5: TEST AND THE ANALOG UNDERLAYER. Complexity is a tax paid in validation. 🔵 $AEHR: silicon photonics test, ramping with the cycle. ' 🔵 $VIAV: every 800G and 1.6T transceiver gets validated before it ships. The gate the market prices like an accessory. 🔵 $SMTC: the drivers and TIAs that fire the lasers. Sits directly under the LPO trade. 🔵 $MTSI: the high-speed analog behind 1.6T engines. 🟠 $CIEN: transport. Even long-haul is buying light. 💡The counter-thesis, because every map needs one. The honest debate on this stack is whether these are genuine bottleneck assets or cyclical optics suppliers enjoying peak demand at peak multiples. Lumentum's May print showed +90% growth with the stock up roughly 1,400% over the prior year at a triple-digit trailing multiple. That is a price for perfection. Most of these names live or die on a handful of hyperscaler capex lines, and one digestion quarter hits the whole stack at once. CPO timing has already slipped once. Architecture risk is real: LPO, CPO, and stretched copper are still fighting for the same sockets. The cycle is real. So is the gravity. 🔥 But the bears have to explain one thing: $NVDA, $MRVL, $CRDO, and $CIEN just spent over $10B securing this supply chain with their own balance sheets. The people with the best information are paying up for the layers. The market owns the top of this stack. The asymmetry is at the edges: wafer, light, packaging, test. Own the layers, not the logo. Bookmark this for the weekend. text: I'm old enough to remember when buying $RIVN was going to be the next big thing.  Which of the photonics stocks is the next Rivian or are they all winners? https://t.co/P02o4huns4

FÁBRICAS EN EL ESPACIO

$SPCX 🚀

En un futuro, con costos de transporte espacial bajos, la fabricación y servicios en órbita se vuelven viables y rentables, especialmente para productos de alto valor donde la ausencia de gravedad mejora significativamente la calidad, reduce defectos por convección/sedimentación o permite estructuras imposibles en Tierra.

Fabricación de Materiales Avanzados

🔸Fibras ópticas ZBLAN (o similares): En microgravedad se producen fibras con mucho menos defectos cristalinos y pérdidas de señal (hasta 10-100 veces mejores que en Tierra). Ideales para telecomunicaciones de alta velocidad, láseres médicos y sensores. Empresas como Flawless Photonics, FOMS y Made In Space ya han demostrado producción en la ISS (kilómetros de fibra).

🔸Cristales de proteínas y farmacéuticos: Crecimiento de cristales más grandes, uniformes y puros para drogas (ej. ritonavir, anticuerpos monoclonales). Mejora la formulación de medicamentos, permite nuevas terapias y acelera el descubrimiento. Varda Space ya retornó productos fabricados en órbita.

🔸Semiconductores y chips: Fabricación con menor contaminación por gravedad, mejor uniformidad en dopaje y cristales. Potencial para superar limitaciones terrestres en rendimiento y escalabilidad (apoyado por CHIPS Act).

🔸Aleaciones y materiales compuestos metálicos: Mezclas más homogéneas sin sedimentación (ej. espumas metálicas, superaleaciones). Mejores propiedades estructurales para aeronáutica, energía y herramientas.

🔸Nanomateriales y vidrios especializados: Producción de nanomateriales, vidrios exóticos o películas delgadas con propiedades únicas gracias al vacío y ausencia de convección.

🔸Impresión 3D y fabricación aditiva en órbita: Partes complejas sin soportes, estructuras grandes (vigas, paneles) optimizadas para espacio. Reduce masa lanzada y permite reparaciones in-situ o construcción de hábitats/telescopios.

🔸Bioprinting y tejidos/regenerativa: Impresión de órganos, retinas artificiales o tejidos en 3D sin colapso por gravedad. LambdaVision y otros trabajan en membranas para restaurar visión.

Servicios y Otras Aplicaciones

🔹Ensamblaje y mantenimiento de grandes estructuras: Construcción de estaciones espaciales, paneles solares orbitales, telescopios gigantes o estaciones de energía solar (Space-Based Solar Power). Más fácil manejar componentes masivos sin peso.

🔹Depósitos de combustible y logística orbital: Fabricación/refinamiento de propelentes o estaciones de servicio para naves (refueling). Clave para misiones profundas.

🔹Investigación y testing de materiales: Laboratorios permanentes para probar comportamientos en microgravedad (fluidos, combustión, biología) que aceleran innovación terrestre.

🔹Producción de productos biológicos y dispositivos médicos: Cultivo de células, modelado de enfermedades o dispositivos implantables con mejor control de fluidos.

🔹Turismo y experiencias en microgravedad: fábricas orbitales podrían ofrecer tours o entornos controlados para entrenamiento/research.

🔹Reciclaje y economía circular orbital: Reprocesar desechos espaciales o satélites en nuevos materiales, reduciendo basura orbital y costos.

Consideraciones Generales

☑️Ventajas clave: Ausencia de convección/burbujas/sedimentación, vacío natural, energía solar abundante y disipación de calor fácil. Ideal para productos de alto valor por kg (fibras ópticas o fármacos justifican fácilmente el transporte).

☑️Desafíos actuales: Retorno seguro a Tierra, automatización/robótica (para reducir tripulación), regulaciones (FDA para fármacos) y escalabilidad. Con costos bajos, se abre un mercado comercial masivo.

☑️Empresas pioneras: Axiom Space, Redwire/Made In Space, Varda, Flawless Photonics, Sierra Space, etc.

Esta lista se basa en avances reales y proyecciones hacia 2030+. Con costos por kg cayendo drásticamente, pasará de nicho a industria transformadora, similar a cómo la reutilización revolucionó los lanzamientos.

text:
$XFAB - The next $TSEM for Europe?

Largest pure-play SiC foundry globally being the only high-volume manufacturing capacity in the US.
 
6 fabs worldwide, and over 400 customers. (#1 foundry supplier for 12 of the top 20)

Sounds incredibly strong at first. But don't be fooled. We are going deeper.

Today, it looks clear that two cycles could be the major upside for this company: power semiconductors and silicon photonics.

1. Power Semis shows direct exposure to $NVDA 800 VDC: $NVTS (heavily relies on them for SiC), and also partners with $POWI and Lite-On.

2. Photonics exposure is through photonixFAB consortium, and three ongoing major projects in development (high-volume production in 2028). $NOK and $NVDA are active evaluators (not confirmed customers yet).

There is also explicit backing through the US and European CHIPS Acts which validates their strategic importance for the West. 

Their tech covers a wide spectrum of CMOS, SOI, SiC, GaN, MEMs, and MTP. They operate on 200 mm wafers and node ranging from 110nm-350nm. They don't compete with customers on IC design, assembly, test or IC sales - they are a pure-play foundry. Partnerships include $IQE, $SOI, Cadence. 

Financially, it has delivered 17% CAGR over the last 5 years. Recent Q1 numbers show revenue growth was down 2%, and that the business is split into automotive (down), industrial (up 32% YoY) and medical (up). Balance sheet is quite stable with $145M in cash and $291M in net debt. 

Melexis (automotive exposure customer) accounts for ~40% of their revenue. Strong baseline but also a concentration factor.

Now here goes comes MY take and view. 

> Technology gap compared to $TSEM: For power semis, their 200mm wafer platform is excellent for high-volume manufacturing. However, for the advanced silicon photonics cycle, tier-1 players prefer migrating toward 300mm wafers to lower optical losses and shrink component footprints. This shows X-FAB is still not the absolute best or preferred platform for the premium photonics cycle yet.

> Growth upside looks heavily weighted towards photonics. Power semis will add solid revenue, but it won't be as explosive as photonics. One reason is because other IDMs like $IFX, $STM, $ON, $WOLF own their own internal fabs and don't rely on X-FAB. Shows they are not the only one in the game or as significant in town for the power semi cycle. We also don't have a lot more details on their three main ongoing photonic projects. So it is hard to be extremely bullish upfront.

> 2026 is going to be a year of steady recovery, and until mid-2027 it doesn't look like the company will show significant revenue growth. Recent Q1 call also looked more bearish than bullish, and it confirmed these timelines. Plenty of time to accumulate shares for those who wish to. Would I suggest that though? Keep reading.

> The downside risk is still really low because Melexis (major revenue stream) is guiding sales growth for H2'26, and with $XFAB at ~1x sales and $1B market cap, the upside is way more here. Seriously way more.

Ultimately, this stock has immense potential. Would definitely be my asymmetrical pick over others that have already run so much like $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, etc. 

It looks like a 1 to 2 year long-term play, and not a quick few-months gain stock. If timelines compress, execution shows better than expected and they continue to improve on their technology sooner, we could easily see the narrative shift rapidly. This looks like something which I am optimistic on happening because that's how quick the AI infrastructure market is going forward overall. Every few months, it just surprises us.

I feel $XFAB can really go 10x or even more in the next few years, but this company needs to execute really well. It still hasn't earned the "next $TSEM" tag, as they aren't worthy of it today.

How would I play this?

- Very worth buying a decent amount of shares here, and holding for the next 2 years. Mainly because upside is 10x+ if things fall in place.

- But I wouldn't go all in at once. Rather build my position slowly and continue to accumulate over time as the story plays out.

NFA. DYOR.

Largest pure-play SiC foundry globally being the only high-volume manufacturing capacity in the US. 6 fabs worldwide, and over 400 customers. (#1 foundry supplier for 12 of the top 20) Sounds incredibly strong at first. But don't be fooled. We are going deeper. Today, it looks clear that two cycles could be the major upside for this company: power semiconductors and silicon photonics. 1. Power Semis shows direct exposure to $NVDA 800 VDC: $NVTS (heavily relies on them for SiC), and also partners with $POWI and Lite-On. 2. Photonics exposure is through photonixFAB consortium, and three ongoing major projects in development (high-volume production in 2028). $NOK and $NVDA are active evaluators (not confirmed customers yet). There is also explicit backing through the US and European CHIPS Acts which validates their strategic importance for the West. Their tech covers a wide spectrum of CMOS, SOI, SiC, GaN, MEMs, and MTP. They operate on 200 mm wafers and node ranging from 110nm-350nm. They don't compete with customers on IC design, assembly, test or IC sales - they are a pure-play foundry. Partnerships include $IQE, $SOI, Cadence. Financially, it has delivered 17% CAGR over the last 5 years. Recent Q1 numbers show revenue growth was down 2%, and that the business is split into automotive (down), industrial (up 32% YoY) and medical (up). Balance sheet is quite stable with $145M in cash and $291M in net debt. Melexis (automotive exposure customer) accounts for ~40% of their revenue. Strong baseline but also a concentration factor. Now here goes comes MY take and view. > Technology gap compared to $TSEM: For power semis, their 200mm wafer platform is excellent for high-volume manufacturing. However, for the advanced silicon photonics cycle, tier-1 players prefer migrating toward 300mm wafers to lower optical losses and shrink component footprints. This shows X-FAB is still not the absolute best or preferred platform for the premium photonics cycle yet. > Growth upside looks heavily weighted towards photonics. Power semis will add solid revenue, but it won't be as explosive as photonics. One reason is because other IDMs like $IFX, $STM, $ON, $WOLF own their own internal fabs and don't rely on X-FAB. Shows they are not the only one in the game or as significant in town for the power semi cycle. We also don't have a lot more details on their three main ongoing photonic projects. So it is hard to be extremely bullish upfront. > 2026 is going to be a year of steady recovery, and until mid-2027 it doesn't look like the company will show significant revenue growth. Recent Q1 call also looked more bearish than bullish, and it confirmed these timelines. Plenty of time to accumulate shares for those who wish to. Would I suggest that though? Keep reading. > The downside risk is still really low because Melexis (major revenue stream) is guiding sales growth for H2'26, and with $XFAB at ~1x sales and $1B market cap, the upside is way more here. Seriously way more. Ultimately, this stock has immense potential. Would definitely be my asymmetrical pick over others that have already run so much like $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, etc. It looks like a 1 to 2 year long-term play, and not a quick few-months gain stock. If timelines compress, execution shows better than expected and they continue to improve on their technology sooner, we could easily see the narrative shift rapidly. This looks like something which I am optimistic on happening because that's how quick the AI infrastructure market is going forward overall. Every few months, it just surprises us. I feel $XFAB can really go 10x or even more in the next few years, but this company needs to execute really well. It still hasn't earned the "next $TSEM" tag, as they aren't worthy of it today. How would I play this? - Very worth buying a decent amount of shares here, and holding for the next 2 years. Mainly because upside is 10x+ if things fall in place. - But I wouldn't go all in at once. Rather build my position slowly and continue to accumulate over time as the story plays out. NFA. DYOR."}}}, "rest_id": "2066281077564383511", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "30539", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

$AAOI seeing heavy upside call flow

Largest order was the 177.5C block (~$611K), with additional buying in 200C/210C/280C/300C strikes

One put sweep at 150P showed up, but overall this is a clear bullish continuation bet

Recent catalyst mix includes AI/photonics momentum, analyst upgrades, and earnings-driven attention

My core holdings right now:

AI infra / neocloud — $NBIS, IREN, NVDA
Fintech / software — HOOD, NOW, CRWD, RDDT
Memory — MU, DRAM
AI power / grid — VST, CEG, VRT, FLNC
Photonics / optical — MRVL, LITE, GLW, COHR
Metals hedge — GLD, SLV calls

Changes since last portfolio post:
Added GLD/SLV call basket.
Added/pressed some NVDA, NBIS, META, NOW, AMZN/VRT call exposure.
Trimmed IREN.
SG is out.
SOXL/SOXS are not core — just tactical trading junk, not a thesis.

Most of the real research here is still around AI infra bottlenecks: neocloud power, memory, photonics, and grid capacity.

But the actual book is broader than this account makes it look: software/fintech and a metals hedge matter too.

The honest state: winners are working, but options/cash leverage is high enough that I'm not trying to broad-add into green tape. New money has to earn it with proof or a pullback.

We'll see which ones earn their way in

text:
“Technical Analysis is Trader’s Astrology” 

Serenity’s Sharp Critique on What Really Moves Markets "White-Haired Stock God" Serenity has sparked debate with a bold take: Technical Analysis (TA) functions more like astrology for traders than a reliable predictive tool. She argues it’s primarily confirmation bias combined with crowd psychology, useful for gauging sentiment and timing entries but not the core driver of prices. Her Key Points: Massive stock surges are rarely caused by chart patterns (golden crosses, dragon candles, etc.).
They are driven by fundamentals and forward expectations. $SIVE +1900%: Market repricing future revenue from $JBL and $GFS catalysts.
$AXTI +8000%: Industry logic including indium phosphide substrates, photonics demand, export controls, and ASP dynamics.

Serenity’s Advice:
TA can help identify psychological entry points, but sustainable price trends are determined by: Industry theme linkages
Earnings expectation changes
Macro environment
Financial report quality
Circulating share structure (float)

Long-term stock potential should be judged by fundamentals and capital structure, not “chart faith. ”Why This Resonates:
In a market full of noise — especially post major IPOs like SpaceX — focusing on real catalysts (revenue growth, competitive advantages, macro tailwinds) provides a clearer edge than pattern chasing.

Investment Takeaway:
This is a timely reminder for traders and investors alike. While charts can be entertaining and occasionally helpful for short-term psychology, the biggest winners come from understanding the underlying business story and capital flows. Bottom Line: Serenity urges a return to basics. In the long run, fundamentals and capital structure trump technical patterns. A healthy dose of skepticism toward “chart magic” can protect capital and improve decision-making. Strong words from a respected voice in the community. 

Do you agree that TA is mostly astrology, or does it still play a key role in your strategy? Drop your thoughts below!

text:
Cheaper tokens ≠ less compute.  
They trigger more compute demand.  

This is Jevons Paradox in AI: cheaper intelligence causes total consumption to explode as new use cases, agentic workflows, and broader adoption become viable.  

Model giants like OpenAI face real price-war pressure. But the picks-and-shovels layer (power, optics, platforms) wins on volume. $IREN , $AAOI , $PLTR and many more are set up beautifully.  

Core insight from recent discussions (Nebius/20VC with Roman Chernin, BG2 Pod, Jordi Visser): every time cost-per-token or cost-per-unit intelligence drops, demand rises, not because people use less, but because they use far more. Previously uneconomic tasks become viable, complex multi-step agentic workflows scale, and new markets open.  

Nebius saw some of its best sales weeks right after major cheap open-source model drops. The market isn’t near its ceiling.  

Jordi Visser laid it out clearly: price compression and commoditization at the model layer (OpenAI/Anthropic facing drastic cuts, 80% of workloads shifting to far cheaper models) versus real inflation and bottlenecks at the physical layer (power, materials, networking, optics). Demand for intelligence is near-infinite. Cheaper per-token pricing unlocks the floodgates on total tokens consumed.  

Who benefits? Model-layer players can get squeezed on margins even as headline revenue grows. The infrastructure and enablement layer feasts on the volume explosion:  

• $IREN — Sustainable power, land, and AI/HPC cloud pivot. Power is the ultimate bottleneck.
• $AAOI — Optical transceivers and photonics for AI data centers. Hyperscalers are ordering 800G+ modules in volume as faster, power-efficient connectivity becomes non-negotiable.  
• $PLTR — Enterprise AI platforms (AIP/Gotham). As agentic workflows and production deployments explode, companies need real software to deploy, govern, and scale AI not just raw GPUs.  

Many more names across power, cooling, networking, and specialized infra will ride the same wave.  

@BG2Pod highlighted the bullish math: monetization per gigawatt is rising, not falling. Demand outstrips supply. Inference revenue is already running well over $200B annualized and climbing fast. Agents will multiply token consumption further. Cheaper tokens plus efficiency gains expand what’s possible within power envelopes, driving more overall buildout.  

This is why the “AI mid-cycle slowdown” narrative is incomplete. It’s healthy digestion and rotation inside a secular bull market. Capital is rotating toward the real beneficiaries of the physical buildout and the software layer that turns raw compute into usable intelligence at scale.  

The compute market remains early. We’re still in the first innings of widespread agentic adoption and enterprise transformation.  

Cheaper intelligence democratizes access → more users and businesses adopt → agents multiply usage per task → total token/compute demand detonates. Model giants feel price pressure. Infra and platform winners feel the volume tailwind.

They trigger more compute demand. This is Jevons Paradox in AI: cheaper intelligence causes total consumption to explode as new use cases, agentic workflows, and broader adoption become viable. Model giants like OpenAI face real price-war pressure. But the picks-and-shovels layer (power, optics, platforms) wins on volume. $IREN , $AAOI , $PLTR and many more are set up beautifully. Core insight from recent discussions (Nebius/20VC with Roman Chernin, BG2 Pod, Jordi Visser): every time cost-per-token or cost-per-unit intelligence drops, demand rises, not because people use less, but because they use far more. Previously uneconomic tasks become viable, complex multi-step agentic workflows scale, and new markets open. Nebius saw some of its best sales weeks right after major cheap open-source model drops. The market isn’t near its ceiling. Jordi Visser laid it out clearly: price compression and commoditization at the model layer (OpenAI/Anthropic facing drastic cuts, 80% of workloads shifting to far cheaper models) versus real inflation and bottlenecks at the physical layer (power, materials, networking, optics). Demand for intelligence is near-infinite. Cheaper per-token pricing unlocks the floodgates on total tokens consumed. Who benefits? Model-layer players can get squeezed on margins even as headline revenue grows. The infrastructure and enablement layer feasts on the volume explosion: • $IREN — Sustainable power, land, and AI/HPC cloud pivot. Power is the ultimate bottleneck. • $AAOI — Optical transceivers and photonics for AI data centers. Hyperscalers are ordering 800G+ modules in volume as faster, power-efficient connectivity becomes non-negotiable. • $PLTR — Enterprise AI platforms (AIP/Gotham). As agentic workflows and production deployments explode, companies need real software to deploy, govern, and scale AI not just raw GPUs. Many more names across power, cooling, networking, and specialized infra will ride the same wave. @BG2Pod highlighted the bullish math: monetization per gigawatt is rising, not falling. Demand outstrips supply. Inference revenue is already running well over $200B annualized and climbing fast. Agents will multiply token consumption further. Cheaper tokens plus efficiency gains expand what’s possible within power envelopes, driving more overall buildout. This is why the “AI mid-cycle slowdown” narrative is incomplete. It’s healthy digestion and rotation inside a secular bull market. Capital is rotating toward the real beneficiaries of the physical buildout and the software layer that turns raw compute into usable intelligence at scale. The compute market remains early. We’re still in the first innings of widespread agentic adoption and enterprise transformation. Cheaper intelligence democratizes access → more users and businesses adopt → agents multiply usage per task → total token/compute demand detonates. Model giants feel price pressure. Infra and platform winners feel the volume tailwind."}}}, "rest_id": "2066252039068262859", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "332", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

SITUATION EXPLAINED: Joshua Levine (.@JoshuaTLevine) of @JoinFAI argues that a quantum buildout with onshore supply chains will drive jobs and growth in the US while also addressing the risks of relying on strategic adversaries.

"The key issue for quantum is the supply chain behind the machines themselves. That includes things like photonics components, rare earth processing, lithium niobate, helium-3, quantum-grade wafers, and advanced packaging.

Some of this should sound familiar, because it overlaps with what we have seen in next-generation chips and chipsets for GPUs, driverless cars, and other modern technologies."

"The difference is the purity level required for quantum systems. Traditional semiconductor manufacturing already requires very high purity, but some quantum systems need even more. We are talking about three to four nines of purity.

text:
Bitcoin is trading near $62k this week, down roughly half from its $130k peak, and the loudest conversions happening right now aren't to crypto. They're from HODLing to trading it like everything else.

The case for never selling rested on one assumption: that time in the asset always beats timing it.

This cycle stress-tested that and the believers blinked. From the October top through the worst week since FTX, the drawdown wiped two years of gains, and the holders who rode it down ate the full opportunity cost: capital frozen in a falling asset while AI and photonics names compounded without them.

Conviction that ignores price isn't a strategy. It's a vow.

The "never get married to an asset" line is the actually durable principle. A thesis is a claim about the world that new facts can amend, and treating any position as un-sellable converts an investment into an identity, which is precisely when people stop seeing the chart in front of them.

The asset doesn't know you're loyal to it.

None of this means the long-term case is dead; it means the holding period became a choice again instead of a religion. Trading in and out has its own graveyard, and most people time it badly. But the cultural shift is real: this drawdown turned diamond hands back into hands.

HODL was a thesis. At half off the top, it's a sunk cost wearing a slogan.

text:
Elon, orbital and lunar data centers may solve the scaling problem, but they may not solve the efficiency problem.

Humanity has spent decades increasing intelligence by adding more silicon, more power, and more cooling. Perhaps the next breakthrough is not bigger data centers, but a different substrate for computation.

We should explore bio-photonic, ionic, and neuromorphic architectures that process information more like living systems than machines. The human brain operates on roughly 20 watts while performing tasks that still challenge our largest AI systems.

Imagine data centers that do not merely consume energy, but recycle heat, capture carbon, produce oxygen, and adapt their internal structure through self-organizing networks.

The long-term future may not be:

Silicon → Bigger Silicon → Space Silicon

but rather:

Electronics → Photonics → Bio-Hybrid Intelligence

If we eventually build AI at planetary or lunar scales, efficiency will become as important as raw computational power. The civilization that learns to compute like biology may outperform the civilization that simply builds larger machines.

The question is not only how much intelligence we can launch into orbit.

The deeper question is:

What is the most efficient form that intelligence can take?

— Mustafa Gökhan Yılmaz
ATHENA / Nexus Symbiosis Research

text:
The reason light always follows me—is because I was being behaviorally controlled. And maybe—I wasn’t someone who ultimately could be controlled.

text:
Every AI wave creates new winners because the bottleneck keeps moving.

First, it was compute.

GPUs → $NVDA $AMD

The market rewarded companies that could train larger models faster.

Then, it was memory bandwidth.

HBM → $MU $SNDK

More GPUs meant more data had to be fed into them.

Then, it became data movement.

Photonics → $AAOI $LITE $COHR

As AI clusters scaled, moving information efficiently between chips and racks became increasingly important.

Now, a new AI bottleneck theme is starting to emerge.

The biggest opportunities often appear where demand is accelerating faster than supply can adapt.

text:
$LITE $COHR $AAOI $AXTI LightCounting releases Q2 Quarterly Market Update – June 2026 @lightcounting 

Q1 2026 was the best quarter for the optical transceiver market since LightCounting started collecting data back in 2004. We estimate the sales of transceivers and AOCs at around $10 billion – up more than 90% from Q1 2025. As illustrated in the figure below, market growth is starting to look truly exponential, as the growth rate has remained above 70% for more than a year.
Our final count for sales of optical transceivers and related products in 2025 stands at $26.8 billion, up 74% from 2024. We were hoping to see some moderation of the growth in 2026, conforming to our soft-landing forecast scenario, but the exponential market trajectory seems relentless. Supply constrains are easing now and this adds gasoline to the fire, not to mention the sky-high spending of the leading cloud companies.
￼
Many companies set new records in Q1 2026, led by Innolight – up 207% y-o-y, as we reported in our early results research note, released on May 14th. Acacia’s record also clearly stands out. Cisco CEO Chuck Robbins commented, “Acacia business had its strongest quarter to date with more than $1 billion in orders in Q3 (calendar Q1) and it is on track to grow over 200% year-over-year in fiscal year '26 (ending in July 2026). Acacia is leading the coherent pluggable optics market, and we see strong momentum across this business.” He also added that “the Acacia business is on fire.” We (LightCounting) have never seen 200% growth in the coherent transceiver market, not since Acacia’s early days in this market.
This report includes historical data and estimates for Q2 2026 on shipments of coherent DWDM ports, including 800ZR/ZR+, which started selling in the end of last year. The ramp in 800ZR/ZR+ has to be phenomenal to achieve 200% growth in Acacia’s business and our estimates for Q2 2026 may be low.
Among many factors that propelled Acacia and Innolight to 200% growth, excellence in supply chain management is really important now, as the industry is hobbled by shortages. Acacia outperformed the competition during the previous cycle, when demand picked up during the COVID shutdown and its supply chain management excellence was a key factor.
Ciena and Nokia are not standing still and both companies improved their supply chain management since COVID. Nokia’s CEO Justin Hotard said that for this year he sees the momentum “on the back of our 800G pluggables and the associated line systems and platforms.” Ciena’s CEO Gary Smith also commented on “strong demand from hyperscalers for our 400G and 800G pluggables, and we remain on track to more than double our pluggable revenue from 2025.” More than doubling may fall below 200%, but it is still remarkable.
A “hidden” factor in Acacia’s success may be its business in selling silicon photonic optical engines to the broader transceiver market where Acacia competes with Intel, Sicoya, Silith and Xphor. According to our recently published report “Silicon Photonics, LPO/LRO and CPO/NPO”, sales of silicon photonics chips will be up 110% in 2026. Most of these chips will be manufactured by Innolight and other transceiver vendors internally (at foundries), but large customers require more diversification. Merchant suppliers of silicon photonics chips may see higher growth in their business, probably reaching close to 200%. There is a significant level of activity in NPO development, which is hard to quantify in terms of sales, but it certainly boosts demand for silicon photonics chips.
Stay tuned for an upcoming LightCounting’s CPO/NPO conference planned for July 15th. A formal announcement will be sent out next week.

text: My theory is this: If robotics are used in behavioral control, then the motions of people who are being controlled would then be robotic. This is me. Notice my robotic movement  (temporal findings on the one second bounce. Notice the light (#photonics) directly on me yet the private jet is in a hangar.  This light is aimed at me to control my movements—and robotics likely are mapping the movement. #behavioralcontrol is not a thing of the future. It’s here. And it was tested on civilians.

text:
Spacex vs Anthropic vs. OpenAI vs. Gemini 

It seems people are realizing none of the frontier models are truly "1 shot" for most tasks and are accepting using chinese models for efficiency might be the right solution to a business problem based on ROI not dreams @ZssBecker mentioned this with his HR department.

I work in AI and I think we will see a commoditization as we realize a perfect frontier model isn't coming and we need cheap tokens for volume, or else we use a chinese model (xD probably a no go at most enterprise but depends on how their security staff likes to roll)

If this ends up being the case, and the "good enough" models commoditize, Spacex orbital data centers become their efficiency source, the other labs will want their own hardware solutions.

I think photonics is their best bet, using light chips could reduce energy per token to 1/100th current costs.

$QCLS and Neurophos are the only  two players here in this niche, early, but huge potential market.  Worth a lotto ticket for anyone imo.

If i'm Anthropic or OpenAI I want to invest asap in Photonic chips and we already see Altman announcing they will compete on cost....there is a way that promise becomes a reality, with profit...thats hardware advances...

The battle isn't over it has just started.

text:
🤖 US startup starts work on ‘first utility-scale quantum computer’ in Australia

American startup PsiQuantum has begun construction on a facility in Moreton Bay, Australia, where it plans to build and deploy what it calls the world’s first utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computer.

Unlike many quantum computing efforts that remain focused on laboratory-scale systems, PsiQuantum is pursuing a photonics-based approach designed to scale using existing semiconductor manufacturing processes and optical networking technologies.

“Building a quantum computer that solves real world problems is one of the great engineering challenges of our time. For decades, quantum computing has held the promise of transforming what humanity can achieve through computation, and today in Australia we are beginning to turn that promise into reality,” — said Victor Peng, CEO of PsiQuantum.

The company believes fault-tolerant quantum computers could eventually tackle problems beyond the reach of conventional computing systems. Potential applications include drug discovery, materials development, energy research, logistics, manufacturing, agriculture, and financial modeling.

For decades, quantum computing has held the promise of transforming what humanity can achieve through computation, and today in Australia we are beginning to turn that promise into reality,” — said Victor Peng, CEO of PsiQuantum. The company believes fault-tolerant quantum computers could eventually tackle problems beyond the reach of conventional computing systems. text:
$MRVL just detailed a major leap in silicon photonics for the AI era. 🔥

Conventional photonic modulators are hitting the diffraction limit of light—they can’t scale fast enough for the insane bandwidth AI clusters will demand.

Plasmonics changes the game. By squeezing light below the diffraction limit at a metal-dielectric interface, Marvell’s team is unlocking speeds >1 THz (10x+ faster than today’s best photonic modulators), devices only ~10 microns long (300–500x smaller), and exponentially higher bandwidth density all inside the existing silicon photonics platform.

No need for exotic new materials. Just smarter physics.

This directly powers the optical interconnects for next-gen scale-up and scale-across AI networks, exactly where Marvell has been building leadership (custom silicon + high-speed optics). They’ve already delivered high-speed SiPho devices with plasmonic tech in 2026, with the roadmap pointing to 3.2T+ modules and big wins in power and space efficiency.

This foundational tech compounds over 5–10 years as AI infrastructure scales. Long-term holders know: connectivity is becoming as critical as the accelerators themselves.

text:
$ALMU - THE PUBLIC PROXY FOR PHOTONICS YOU CAN'T BUY IN ANDURIL OR LIGHTMATTER, STILL PRE-REVENUE

Aeluma rose ~6% to $25 as the AI-optics complex snapped back from yesterday's rate flush. Its pitch: manufacturing III-V compound semiconductors — the lasers and detectors photonics needs — directly on 300mm silicon wafers with CMOS-compatible processes, partnered with Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo, with quantum-dot lasers aimed at data-center co-packaged optics.

Be honest about the stage: fiscal-2026 revenue is guided to just $4.2–4.6M from R&D contracts, not production hardware, with no named commercial design win yet. This is a call option on an inevitability — the bet that scalable III-V-on-silicon is the missing manufacturing layer when CPO goes volume — not a revenue story today.

text:
$AEHR - THE BURN-IN BOTTLENECK NOBODY PRICES UNTIL AI CHIPS START FAILING IN THE FIELD

Aehr Test Systems rose ~7% to $113 as investors re-rated its wafer-level burn-in franchise into a William Blair presentation and a fresh June deck mapping AI-processor and data-center demand. The company already booked a record $41M production order from a leading hyperscale AI customer this spring and is now chasing a development deal for HBM4E memory-optimized blades, targeting fiscal-2027 orders.

The mechanism is reliability at scale: as SiC, GaN, photonics and HBM go into multi-thousand-GPU racks, every die has to be stressed before it ships — and Aehr is the lone pure-play in wafer-level burn-in across all four, the second derivative of the AI buildout.

text:
$NOK

Nokia is a screaming buy right now

The company has unveiled plans to expand its photonic chip facility in Allentown, Pennsylvania with the additional capacity expected to be operational by the end of Q3 2026

The fact that you can still buy this stock at $13 tells me the market still treats this company as a mobile phone business 

Nokia has taken control of one of the most significant photonics manufacturing facilities in the United States and is now driving a tenfold scale up that is backed by government funding

This stock should easily be $30

text:
🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — June 17, 2026

📊 SMH: $623.97 (+1.29%)

📈 Earnings:
• JBL — $8.8B rev (est $8.66B), $3.16 core EPS. Core operating margin 5.7%, +12% YoY. Raised FY26 outlook to $35B revenue, 5.8% core operating margin, $12.70 core EPS, and $1.4B+ adjusted FCF as AI infrastructure demand stayed extremely strong. Stock finished +1.9%.

🔥 Major Developments:
• AEHR received another follow-on production order for a fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in system from a major silicon photonics customer serving hyperscale data-center optical interconnects, with additional systems forecast this year as next-gen capacity ramps.
• Intel’s 18A-P process entered risk production, moving its most advanced foundry node closer to external customer qualification and keeping the Apple/advanced-node second-source debate alive.
• NVIDIA Blackwell added day-zero support for MiniMax M3, a 428B-parameter MoE model with 1M-token context, reinforcing the push toward high-throughput agentic inference on Blackwell systems.
• AI infrastructure breadth kept widening: Jabil raised its FY26 outlook on hyperscale demand, while Aehr’s optical burn-in order points to production bottlenecks moving deeper into photonics reliability, test, and manufacturing capacity.

From raw materials to finished systems, here’s how the entire photonics stack is positioned to benefit from exploding demand in AI data centers, optical networking, and high-speed computing.

Layer 1 - Materials & Wafers
 $GLW $AXTI $IQE $AIXA $AMS

Layer 2 - Core Photonic Devices
 $IPGP $COHR $LITE $LASR $SIVE

Layer 3 - Components & Modules
 $AAOI $MTSI $FN $VIAV $LPTH

Layer 4 - Systems & Equipment
 $ASML $BESI $ASM $LPKF $MKS

Layer 5 - Test, Metrology & Yield
 $CAMT $FORM $AEHR $ONTO $VIAV 

Photonics is critical for moving massive amounts of data at the speed of light, exactly what hyperscalers need as AI training scales. 

Many of these names are flying under the radar but sit at the heart of the next leg of AI infrastructure spend.

text:
Very strong results from $JBL in Q3 FY2026

They reported $8.8B in revenue and core EPS of $3.16

Beating market expectations and raising guidance for the fourth quarter

The most relevant part is that they highlighted strong demand in AI infrastructure

Which led them to increase their annual revenue projection for this segment to $13.6B

This is excellent news for the entire supply chain

Especially for companies like $SIVE and $AAOI 

IMO these numbers act as a positive catalyst for $SIVE within a clearly bullish environment for the photonics sector and hyperscalers capex

Very bullish on $SIVE anda $AAOI

text:
I’ve been saying for a while that energy is the next bottleneck after memory/photonics. 

The companies that are able to provide and lend the energy will see MASSIVE returns over the coming years. 

Energy is going to be the most critical component of advancing our infrastructure, whether it is for AI or not. 

Now $NVDA is telling you that energy is more critical than you can imagine. What will you do about it anon?

$SLNH $SMR $ENPH $TE $NUAI

text:
Everything about my investing strategy, capital, and performance since I started investing a year and a half ago.

My investing journey

I started 2025 with around $3.5k, mostly index fund maxxxing.

Then I got wrecked by the Trump market volatility and entered 2026 with around $18.5k and a -5% performance.

Six months later, my portfolio is sitting at roughly $46k . Around $30k came from monthly DCA, but the rest came from performance.

My current goal is to reach $100k by the end of the year while keeping my DCA much lower than in year one. I’m no longer trying to save every penny. Instead, I’m focusing on two things : building a high-conviction bull-growth investing strategy and increasing my income.

I believe the AI supercycle, aka stock market in easy mode, still has a few years left before we return to hard mode.

My strategy is simple : identify businesses positioned to capture a meaningful share of the AI infrastructure buildout, especially in capital-intensive markets where contracts, scale, and execution can quickly reprice a company.

The goal is to maximize returns through short to medium-term asymmetric bets, while holding my highest-conviction names as long as the thesis continues to play out.

Current holdings and 2026 price targets

$IREN : End-of-year PT: $140 (+237%)

IREN is my largest position, representing around 73% of my portfolio.

The stock has underperformed over the last six months, but my conviction remains very high. I believe the next wave of contracts, improved economics, and continued scaling could push the stock significantly higher.

This is my highest-conviction AI infrastructure bet. I plan to hold and watch the company scale until at least 2030, as long as the thesis keeps playing out.

$NUAI : End-of-year PT : $15 (+300%)

NUAI represents around 12% of my portfolio.

The company has built a very strong team and is positioning itself for a potential hyperscaler deal around its 650MW Texas campus.

This single event could potentially double the stock’s value, especially considering the company is currently valued at around $500M .

$SIVE : End-of-year PT : 500 SEK (+500%)

Sivers represents around 10% of my portfolio.

This is a much more speculative position, but the upside could be massive. If Sivers manages to capture a meaningful share of future photonics demand, I don’t see how the company stays below a $3B market cap.

The risk is high, but so is the potential reward.

$AMPG : End-of-year PT : $40 (+500%)

AMPG represents around 5% of my portfolio.

This is my moonshot / for-fun position, built from profits made on a recent swing trade. I haven’t done as much research on this company as I have on my core positions, but the growth potential is serious.

The company could become increasingly involved in contracts with hyperscale clients, which makes it an interesting high-risk, high-reward bet.

Final thoughts

My portfolio is extremely concentrated and clearly not built for everyone.

But I’m young, I have high risk tolerance, and I believe this market still offers many asymmetric opportunities for investors willing to do their D.D and not afraid of volatility.

The objective is to aggressively compound capital during what I believe is the biggest infrastructure buildouts of the century.

My investing journey I started 2025 with around $3.5k, mostly index fund maxxxing. Then I got wrecked by the Trump market volatility and entered 2026 with around $18.5k and a -5% performance. Six months later, my portfolio is sitting at roughly $46k . Around $30k came from monthly DCA, but the rest came from performance. My current goal is to reach $100k by the end of the year while keeping my DCA much lower than in year one. I’m no longer trying to save every penny. Instead, I’m focusing on two things : building a high-conviction bull-growth investing strategy and increasing my income. I believe the AI supercycle, aka stock market in easy mode, still has a few years left before we return to hard mode. My strategy is simple : identify businesses positioned to capture a meaningful share of the AI infrastructure buildout, especially in capital-intensive markets where contracts, scale, and execution can quickly reprice a company. The goal is to maximize returns through short to medium-term asymmetric bets, while holding my highest-conviction names as long as the thesis continues to play out. Current holdings and 2026 price targets $IREN : End-of-year PT: $140 (+237%) IREN is my largest position, representing around 73% of my portfolio. The stock has underperformed over the last six months, but my conviction remains very high. I believe the next wave of contracts, improved economics, and continued scaling could push the stock significantly higher. This is my highest-conviction AI infrastructure bet. I plan to hold and watch the company scale until at least 2030, as long as the thesis keeps playing out. $NUAI : End-of-year PT : $15 (+300%) NUAI represents around 12% of my portfolio. The company has built a very strong team and is positioning itself for a potential hyperscaler deal around its 650MW Texas campus. This single event could potentially double the stock’s value, especially considering the company is currently valued at around $500M . $SIVE : End-of-year PT : 500 SEK (+500%) Sivers represents around 10% of my portfolio. This is a much more speculative position, but the upside could be massive. If Sivers manages to capture a meaningful share of future photonics demand, I don’t see how the company stays below a $3B market cap. The risk is high, but so is the potential reward. $AMPG : End-of-year PT : $40 (+500%) AMPG represents around 5% of my portfolio. This is my moonshot / for-fun position, built from profits made on a recent swing trade. I haven’t done as much research on this company as I have on my core positions, but the growth potential is serious. The company could become increasingly involved in contracts with hyperscale clients, which makes it an interesting high-risk, high-reward bet. Final thoughts My portfolio is extremely concentrated and clearly not built for everyone. But I’m young, I have high risk tolerance, and I believe this market still offers many asymmetric opportunities for investors willing to do their D.D and not afraid of volatility. The objective is to aggressively compound capital during what I believe is the biggest infrastructure buildouts of the century."}}}, "rest_id": "2065903808245317656", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "143", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

$POET is one I’m watching here.

It’s a photonics / optical interconnect name tied to AI data center connectivity, basically the type of tech needed to move data faster and more efficiently.

After a quick rally, POET pulled back into key support and held.

On the higher timeframe, this still looks like a bull flag.

Price bounced off the Ichimoku cloud and is now trying to reclaim the fast momentum cloud.

If it breaks out of this flag, this could set up for another leg higher.

text: @CKCapitalxx whos saying that lol. everyone on my feed sounds like they want to liquidate their 401k to ape in on more photonics

𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝒄𝒉𝒊𝒑𝒔 𝒘𝒆𝒓𝒆 𝒋𝒖𝒔𝒕 𝒄𝒉𝒂𝒑𝒕𝒆𝒓 𝒐𝒏𝒆.

The real constraints on scaling AI are no longer measured in teraflops alone. They’re measured in megawatts you can actually deliver, photons you can move at rack scale, liquid you can pump without melting the floor, and platforms that turn raw infrastructure into deployed, governed intelligence.

Jensen has been clear for months: the handoff is happening. Copper has limits. Power is the new gate. Cooling density is non-negotiable. The companies securing these layers with long-term contracts and balance-sheet capital are building durable positions.

Here’s how the 2026 AI infrastructure stack breaks down from silicon to intelligence:

LAYER 1: SILICON FOUNDATION  
🟠 $TSM  — The advanced foundry and packaging backbone (CoWoS and beyond). This is the capacity gate for the highest-performance AI chips. Everything upstream starts here.

LAYER 2: AI COMPUTE  
🟠 $NVDA  — Still the clear leader in GPUs and full AI systems/racks. The ecosystem investments and rack-level selling show they understand the game has moved beyond individual chips.

LAYER 3: SERVERS & RACK INTEGRATION  
🔵 $SMCI — Building dense, liquid-ready AI server and rack systems that hyperscalers deploy at scale. Direct volume beneficiary of the buildout.

LAYER 4: NETWORKING & HIGH-SPEED INTERCONNECT  
🔵 $MRVL  — Switching silicon, DSPs, and aggressive silicon photonics push via Celestial AI. Essential for scaling clusters beyond single racks.  
🔵 $AAOI  — Datacom optics leader with explosive data center revenue growth and a major 800G/1.6T order backlog. Scaling domestic capacity while supply chain security matters.

LAYER 5: PHOTONICS & OPTICS TRANSITION  
The physics is clear, copper hits the wall on bandwidth density and power. Photonics moves from “nice to have” to required for the next jump in cluster scale.  
🔵 $LITE (Lumentum) — Leading provider of high-speed lasers, EMLs, and optical components with a major NVIDIA strategic investment and multi-billion dollar purchase commitments. Scaling US manufacturing for AI data center optics demand.

LAYER 6: POWER: THE BINDING CONSTRAINT  
🟠 $VST — Signing multi-decade power purchase agreements directly with hyperscalers. Nuclear uprates and reliable generation capacity are becoming strategic assets.  
🔵 $IREN  — Renewable-powered data centers executing a clear pivot into AI/HPC cloud. Large-scale sites with secured power and flexible capacity. 5.8 GW-scale pipeline building.

LAYER 7: COOLING & THERMAL MANAGEMENT  
🔵 $VRT  — Liquid cooling systems are moving from pilot to must-have for high-density AI racks. Record backlog and order growth as power density per rack climbs.

LAYER 8: CLOUD & DATA CENTER PLATFORMS  
🔵 $ORCL  — Oracle Cloud Infrastructure is seeing massive AI-driven demand. Major capex ramp, OpenAI/Stargate ecosystem work, and enormous remaining performance obligations.  
🔵 $NBIS  — AI infrastructure specialist rapidly scaling contracted capacity (multi-GW targets). Major hyperscaler deals are driving strong ARR acceleration.

LAYER 9: THE INTELLIGENCE LAYER  
🔵 $PLTR  — Palantir’s AIP platform turns physical infrastructure plus enterprise data into production-grade, governed AI applications. U.S. commercial momentum is accelerating sharply.

Capital is already voting with real money: direct hyperscaler power deals, NVIDIA’s multi-billion dollar optics investments, pre-payments for capacity years out, and software platforms seeing accelerating commercial traction.

The honest counter-thesis is that real valuations across parts of the stack are elevated, power timelines can slip, cooling and photonics tech is still maturing in some deployments. Software ROI takes time to prove at scale. One digestive quarter can pressure the whole chain. But the structural, multi-year spend from the largest balance sheets in tech is not a narrative. It’s signed contracts and capex plans.

The market has aggressively priced the obvious chip layer. The asymmetry sits in the scarce layers everything else depends on.

Own the full stack. Not just the logo on the GPU.

Bookmark this. Then drop which layer you think moves first or which one I’m underweighting.

Tag the person who’s still only buying NVDA and calling it a diversified AI portfolio.

text:
The Copper-to-Light Shift: Why Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVE is sitting right in the middle of the perfect AI storm

We’re standing at a shift that reminds me strongly of when the world moved from copper to fiber in broadband. Back then, many smart people looked at the situation and said “this is good enough – we don’t need to change everything.” History proved them wrong in a spectacular way. Fiber didn’t just improve the internet – it completely unlocked streaming, cloud computing, social media, and the entire digital society we take for granted today.

Now the same story is repeating itself, only this time the stakes are dramatically higher.

Copper has reached its physical and economic limit.

AI is growing at a pace we’ve never seen. Massive clusters with tens of thousands – soon hundreds of thousands – of GPUs that all need to communicate with each other at extreme speeds, constantly. Moore’s Law has kept delivering smaller, faster and more power-efficient transistors on the chips themselves, but the interconnects – the actual nervous system that lets all these chips talk to one another – are stuck in the past. Copper simply can’t handle it anymore. We see signal degradation over distance, enormous heat generation, sky-high energy consumption, and physical scaling problems that get worse with every new generation of AI. At the same time, the copper price itself is surging because of AI demand and global electrification. Building data centers the old way isn’t just becoming technically challenging anymore – it’s becoming economically absurd.

So what’s the alternative?

Honestly, there aren’t many good ones left.

We have to move to light.

Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) – where the optics and lasers are placed right next to the chips – changes the game completely. Dramatically lower power consumption, vastly higher bandwidth, and scalability that copper can never match. The biggest hyperscalers and chip companies are already pouring billions into this technology. This isn’t a nice-to-have. It’s a survival requirement for the next generation of AI. Without this shift we will simply hit a hard wall in how far AI can advance.

Just like fiber laid the foundation for today’s digital world, CPO and photonics in general will become the invisible backbone of tomorrow’s AI infrastructure. There is no realistic way around it.

And sitting right in the middle of this perfect storm is Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVEF.

At the very heart of the CPO revolution you need reliable, high-performance laser light sources that can feed these new optical engines efficiently. That is exactly Sivers’ strongest area with their advanced DFB laser arrays. They are already partnering closely with the companies that are building the actual next-generation infrastructure: $GFS and their SCALE CPO platform (targeting a $25 billion pluggable optics market by 2030), $POET, O-Net, $ENA.NE and several others for scalable External Laser Source solutions. 

They combine this with an asset-light model that gives them speed and flexibility to scale without massive capital expenditure, a clear technical edge in customized lasers, proven working demos together with players like Ayar Labs, and natural exposure to AI data centers, SATCOM and defense – markets that all need precisely this kind of photonics.

The broader shift from copper to light in AI infrastructure is heading toward tens of billions of dollars in the coming years. Sivers is positioned where the critical technology meets exploding demand. 

During CMD 2025, CEO Vickram Vathulya highlighted from customer conversations that Sivers seems to be the only ones capable of providing laser arrays in the exact formats and power levels that customers need for these new architectures. A few others do laser arrays, but at much lower power levels.

CTO Andrew McKee added even more color at the same event: 2027 is the target for volume production, with qualification starting this year and pilot ramp-up in 2026. Looking at 2028 forecasts of 19 million AI GPU shipments, and typical architectures needing 5–16 laser sources per GPU, we’re talking hundreds of millions of laser sources per year. An extremely large and exciting addressable market as this rolls out in the data centers.

This isn’t just another incremental upgrade. It’s a fundamental infrastructure transformation.

The ones who understand this early will see why photonics is becoming one of the clearest and strongest tailwinds in the entire AI value chain.

$SIVE feels extremely well positioned.

Back then, many smart people looked at the situation and said “this is good enough – we don’t need to change everything.” History proved them wrong in a spectacular way. Fiber didn’t just improve the internet – it completely unlocked streaming, cloud computing, social media, and the entire digital society we take for granted today. Now the same story is repeating itself, only this time the stakes are dramatically higher. Copper has reached its physical and economic limit. AI is growing at a pace we’ve never seen. Massive clusters with tens of thousands – soon hundreds of thousands – of GPUs that all need to communicate with each other at extreme speeds, constantly. Moore’s Law has kept delivering smaller, faster and more power-efficient transistors on the chips themselves, but the interconnects – the actual nervous system that lets all these chips talk to one another – are stuck in the past. Copper simply can’t handle it anymore. We see signal degradation over distance, enormous heat generation, sky-high energy consumption, and physical scaling problems that get worse with every new generation of AI. At the same time, the copper price itself is surging because of AI demand and global electrification. Building data centers the old way isn’t just becoming technically challenging anymore – it’s becoming economically absurd. So what’s the alternative? Honestly, there aren’t many good ones left. We have to move to light. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) – where the optics and lasers are placed right next to the chips – changes the game completely. Dramatically lower power consumption, vastly higher bandwidth, and scalability that copper can never match. The biggest hyperscalers and chip companies are already pouring billions into this technology. This isn’t a nice-to-have. It’s a survival requirement for the next generation of AI. Without this shift we will simply hit a hard wall in how far AI can advance. Just like fiber laid the foundation for today’s digital world, CPO and photonics in general will become the invisible backbone of tomorrow’s AI infrastructure. There is no realistic way around it. And sitting right in the middle of this perfect storm is Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVEF. At the very heart of the CPO revolution you need reliable, high-performance laser light sources that can feed these new optical engines efficiently. That is exactly Sivers’ strongest area with their advanced DFB laser arrays. They are already partnering closely with the companies that are building the actual next-generation infrastructure: $GFS and their SCALE CPO platform (targeting a $25 billion pluggable optics market by 2030), $POET, O-Net, $ENA.NE and several others for scalable External Laser Source solutions. They combine this with an asset-light model that gives them speed and flexibility to scale without massive capital expenditure, a clear technical edge in customized lasers, proven working demos together with players like Ayar Labs, and natural exposure to AI data centers, SATCOM and defense – markets that all need precisely this kind of photonics. The broader shift from copper to light in AI infrastructure is heading toward tens of billions of dollars in the coming years. Sivers is positioned where the critical technology meets exploding demand. During CMD 2025, CEO Vickram Vathulya highlighted from customer conversations that Sivers seems to be the only ones capable of providing laser arrays in the exact formats and power levels that customers need for these new architectures. A few others do laser arrays, but at much lower power levels. CTO Andrew McKee added even more color at the same event: 2027 is the target for volume production, with qualification starting this year and pilot ramp-up in 2026. Looking at 2028 forecasts of 19 million AI GPU shipments, and typical architectures needing 5–16 laser sources per GPU, we’re talking hundreds of millions of laser sources per year. An extremely large and exciting addressable market as this rolls out in the data centers. This isn’t just another incremental upgrade. It’s a fundamental infrastructure transformation. The ones who understand this early will see why photonics is becoming one of the clearest and strongest tailwinds in the entire AI value chain. $SIVE feels extremely well positioned."}}}, "rest_id": "2065889424605618466", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11403", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Most people still don’t see it. Photonics is where Memory was a year ago. That’s why we’re still VERY early. The pattern is clear if you’ve been watching this AI super cycle unfold since 2022. > Chips kicked everything off after ChatGPT dropped in late 2022/early 2023. $NVDA went nuclear with Hopper H100s, and then Blackwell came. $AMD started carving out real share in GPUs and custom ASICs. > Memory followed with a lag. Mid-2024 onward, HBM went into massive shortage as AI training and inference sucked up capacity. That pulled DRAM and NAND higher too. Micron $MU, SK Hynix, Samsung and other memory players delivered strong re-ratings as pricing power returned. > Now in mid-2026, memory is deep in its prolonged supercycle - sold out for years ahead (confirmed by Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra). Chips are in a more mature phase, still growing strong with 1:1 CPU to GPU ratios as AI agents scale, and will sustain into the second half of this decade as Lisa Su says compute capacity will 100x by 2030. But what’s next? Photonics, folks! - AI clusters are suffering from constraints related to power, heat, latency and bandwidth. Optics solves this. That is why the next MAJOR leg is clearly SiPh and CPO. - 1.6T pluggables are ramping, early CPO deployments are starting but nowhere near to the scale they will reach because real mass adoption and deep in-package integration will come in 2027-2028+. - Goldman Sach predicts TAM will explode 10x by the end of this decade. To better understand this, if one divides it into 3 common phases across the supercycles (Early, Acceleration, Maturity), we see that: - Chips are clearly in Phase 3 - Memory is in Phase 2/3 - Photonics is very early in Phase 2 🔥 The AI buildout keeps shifting to the next bottleneck because the world needs more compute than ever before. This isn’t slowing down anytime soon! text:
Let me let you guys in on a secret that can help you catch a lot of these moves before they happen so you're not stuck chasing and holding the bag.

Listen up.

Create a watchlist with the leaders in every sector that's been hot this year.

For example: Photonics, Semis, FAANG, Space, Robotics, Financials, Healthcare, Energy, etc.

Money is constantly rotating between these sectors. Your job is to determine where institutional money is positioned before each session.

Within those sector leaders, you'll have a group of smaller names that align with the same theme.

Something that really stood out to me last Friday was how memory names started bidding aggressively despite a weak tape. Those are some of my favorite setups because if the market decides to turn around, those names are usually the first to lead.

Between Thursday and Friday of last week, $SNDK ran from 1670 to 2000+.

While that name can be difficult for traders with smaller accounts to participate in, there are other stocks within the exact same theme that often make aggressive moves shortly after.

My focus immediately shifted to $WDC and $STX.

$WDC ran from 500 to 730 in just 3 sessions (+46%).

$STX ran from 870 to 1100 in just 3 sessions (+26%).

While many traders wait for 10 indicators to trigger before hitting the buy button and end up chasing these moves, there are other ways to get ahead.

The market leaves clues. You just have to know where to look.

NOTE: $COHR is the largest position in my "Photonics Is Next" portfolio on @joinautopilot followed by $30M.

text:
AI infrastructure runs on more than compute. It runs on light.

Today in Sherman, Texas, Jensen Huang joined @CoherentCorp CEO Jim Anderson and local leaders to mark the expansion of U.S. manufacturing capacity for advanced photonics.

The technology is behind the lasers and optical links that move data across modern AI systems, strengthening the American manufacturing base behind the AI era.

text:
🚨 SCIENTISTS JUST ACHIEVED OPTICAL MULTISTABILITY IN A TINY MICROCAVITY  A BIG STEP FOR PHOTONIC MEMORY.

In a new paper published in Nature Nanotechnology, researchers demonstrated multiple stable optical states inside a compact microcavity by using near-exceptional coupling. 

This allows the device to “remember” different light intensities as distinct, stable states  something normally only possible in much larger or more complex systems.
Optical multistability is a key requirement for multilevel optical memory and all-optical computing. Until now, achieving it in a small enough footprint for on-chip use has been extremely difficult because optical nonlinearities are usually very weak.

Why this matters:

• It brings us closer to practical on-chip optical memory that can store multiple bits of information per device
• Exceptional-point physics can dramatically enhance effective nonlinearity without needing exotic materials
• Compact multistable devices could enable faster, more energy-efficient photonic processors
• It shows how non-Hermitian physics is becoming a powerful design tool in nanophotonics

The deeper implication:

We’re learning to engineer light itself to behave in increasingly complex and useful ways inside tiny structures. By operating near exceptional points, researchers can coax weak optical nonlinearities into producing strong, multistable behavior.

 This kind of advance moves photonic computing from theory toward something that can actually be fabricated at scale on a chip.

If this approach can be further optimized and integrated with existing silicon photonics, it could become a building block for future light-based memory and logic.

How do you think compact optical multistability could accelerate the development of photonic computers?

Follow for more frontier nanophotonics, exceptional-point physics, and optical computing.

In a new paper published in Nature Nanotechnology, researchers demonstrated multiple stable optical states inside a compact microcavity by using near-exceptional coupling. This allows the device to “remember” different light intensities as distinct, stable states something normally only possible in much larger or more complex systems. Optical multistability is a key requirement for multilevel optical memory and all-optical computing. Until now, achieving it in a small enough footprint for on-chip use has been extremely difficult because optical nonlinearities are usually very weak. Why this matters: • It brings us closer to practical on-chip optical memory that can store multiple bits of information per device • Exceptional-point physics can dramatically enhance effective nonlinearity without needing exotic materials • Compact multistable devices could enable faster, more energy-efficient photonic processors • It shows how non-Hermitian physics is becoming a powerful design tool in nanophotonics The deeper implication: We’re learning to engineer light itself to behave in increasingly complex and useful ways inside tiny structures. By operating near exceptional points, researchers can coax weak optical nonlinearities into producing strong, multistable behavior. This kind of advance moves photonic computing from theory toward something that can actually be fabricated at scale on a chip. If this approach can be further optimized and integrated with existing silicon photonics, it could become a building block for future light-based memory and logic. How do you think compact optical multistability could accelerate the development of photonic computers? Nokia in Allentown, PA — 10x capacity for photonics packaging.

Copper is dying. Photonics is almost ready. Almost has been a great year for photonics. Every year.
